concepts_list.name: rho
concepts_list.description: Fluid density, a scalar quantity that appears in the momentum and mass balance equations.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol ρ is explicitly defined as the fluid density in the problem statement and appears in both the momentum and mass equations (Eq. (1) and (2)).

concepts_list.name: mu
concepts_list.description: Dynamic viscosity of the fluid, which may depend on the shear rate and is a key parameter in the stress tensor formulation.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol μ is explicitly referred to as the dynamic viscosity and appears in the stress tensor expression (Eq. (3)) and in the Carreau model (Eq. (4)).

concepts_list.name: t
concepts_list.description: Pseudo-traction vector on the Neumann boundary, representing the surface force applied on the boundary Γ_N.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol t is defined as a pseudo-traction on Γ_N in the boundary condition (Eq. (7)) and appears in the weak formulation (Eq. (8)) as a boundary source term.

concepts_list.name: w_h
concepts_list.description: Test function for the velocity field in the finite element discretization, belonging to the discrete velocity space.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol w_h appears multiple times as a test function in the weak formulation (Eqs. (8) and (9)), with subscript h indicating its finite element discretized form, and is used in the inner product with u_h and p_h.

concepts_list.name: u_h
concepts_list.description: Discretized velocity field in the finite element method, representing the approximate solution to the fluid velocity.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol u_h appears repeatedly in the weak formulation (Eqs. (8) and (9)) as the primary unknown and is used in both the momentum and incompressibility equations, with subscript h denoting the finite element approximation.

concepts_list.name: p_h
concepts_list.description: Discretized pressure field in the finite element method, representing the approximate solution to the pressure.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol p_h appears in the weak formulation (Eqs. (8) and (9)) as the pressure variable and is used in the divergence-free constraint and stabilization terms, with subscript h indicating finite element discretization.

concepts_list.name: Gamma_N
concepts_list.description: Part of the boundary Γ where Neumann boundary conditions are applied, corresponding to the traction boundary.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol Γ_N is explicitly defined as a part of the boundary Γ where Neumann boundary conditions are imposed (Eq. (7)) and appears in the weak formulation as the domain of the boundary integral.

concepts_list.name: nabla_s
concepts_list.description: Symmetric gradient operator, defined as half the sum of the gradient and its transpose, used to compute the shear rate and stress tensor.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol ∇s appears in the definition of the shear rate (Eq. (6)) and in the stress tensor (Eq. (3)), where it is applied to the velocity field. It is explicitly defined as ∇u + (∇u)^T over 2, indicating it is a symmetric gradient operator.

rho.description:
Fluid density, a scalar quantity that appears in the momentum and mass balance equations.
JUSTIFICATION:
The symbol ρ is explicitly defined as the fluid density in the problem statement and appears in both the momentum and mass equations (Eq. (1) and (2)).

mu.description:
Dynamic viscosity of the fluid, which may depend on the shear rate and is a key parameter in the stress tensor formulation.
JUSTIFICATION:
The symbol μ is explicitly referred to as the dynamic viscosity and appears in the stress tensor expression (Eq. (3)) and in the Carreau model (Eq. (4)).

t.description:
Pseudo-traction vector on the Neumann boundary, representing the surface force applied on the boundary Γ_N.
JUSTIFICATION:
The symbol t is defined as a pseudo-traction on Γ_N in the boundary condition (Eq. (7)) and appears in the weak formulation (Eq. (8)) as a boundary source term.

w_h.description:
Test function for the velocity field in the finite element discretization, belonging to the discrete velocity space.
JUSTIFICATION:
The symbol w_h appears multiple times as a test function in the weak formulation (Eqs. (8) and (9)), with subscript h indicating its finite element discretized form, and is used in the inner product with u_h and p_h.

u_h.description:
Discretized velocity field in the finite element method, representing the approximate solution to the fluid velocity.
JUSTIFICATION:
The symbol u_h appears repeatedly in the weak formulation (Eqs. (8) and (9)) as the primary unknown and is used in both the momentum and incompressibility equations, with subscript h denoting the finite element approximation.

p_h.description:
Discretized pressure field in the finite element method, representing the approximate solution to the pressure.
JUSTIFICATION:
The symbol p_h appears in the weak formulation (Eqs. (8) and (9)) as the pressure variable and is used in the divergence-free constraint and stabilization terms, with subscript h indicating finite element discretization.

Gamma_N.description:
Part of the boundary Γ where Neumann boundary conditions are applied, corresponding to the traction boundary.
JUSTIFICATION:
The symbol Γ_N is explicitly defined as a part of the boundary Γ where Neumann boundary conditions are imposed (Eq. (7)) and appears in the weak formulation as the domain of the boundary integral.

nabla_s.description:
Symmetric gradient operator, defined as half the sum of the gradient and its transpose, used to compute the shear rate and stress tensor.
JUSTIFICATION:
The symbol ∇s appears in the definition of the shear rate (Eq. (6)) and in the stress tensor (Eq. (3)), where it is applied to the velocity field. It is explicitly defined as ∇u + (∇u)^T over 2, indicating it is a symmetric gradient operator.
